walkthrough.title: Place the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Great Western Trail. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. This board shows the trail from Texas to Kansas City, with branching paths, building spaces, and the railroad running along the top. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Place Station Masters
walkthrough.text: Find the five station master tiles. Shuffle them and place one face up on each station master space along the railroad track at the top of the board. These provide powerful bonuses when you upgrade your train to reach their stations.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Job Market
walkthrough.text: Place the job market token on the top dashed round space. Now sort the fifty-four worker tiles by back number. You will see ones, twos, and threes. Shuffle each pile separately. Fill the job market from the starting column for your player count to the space left of the job market token. Workers come in three types: cowboys, craftsmen, and engineers.

walkthrough.title: Place Neutral Buildings
walkthrough.text: Find the seven neutral building tiles labeled A through G. For your first game, place them on the matching spaces printed on the board. In later games, you can shuffle them and randomize their placement for more variety. These buildings provide actions when any player stops on them.

walkthrough.title: Sort and Place Hazard and Teepee Tiles
walkthrough.text: Separate the hazard tiles and teepee tiles by their back numbers. Shuffle each pile face down. Hazards include floods, droughts, and rockfalls. Teepees come in blue and green. Reveal back-one tiles and place them on the board sections until seven total tiles have entered the trail. Then fill the foresight row with two tiles from each back number.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Objective Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the twenty-four objective cards and reveal four face up into the general objective display. These show end-game scoring conditions that any player can work toward. Set the rest of the deck nearby.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Cattle Market
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the thirty-six market cattle cards. Reveal cards into the cattle market display based on your player count. For two players, reveal seven cards. For three players, reveal ten. For four players, reveal thirteen. Arrange them left to right by color: yellow, red, blue, brown, purple.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Bank
walkthrough.text: Sort the coins into piles of one-dollar and five-dollar denominations. Place them within reach of all players. This is the bank. You will be buying and selling throughout the game, so keep the coins accessible.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a color and takes the matching player board. You also take ten private building tiles numbered one through ten. For your first game, set all buildings to the A side. In later games, you can randomize which sides face up.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Player Board
walkthrough.text: Place fourteen discs of your color on the colored spaces of your player board, leaving the two gray spaces empty. These discs cover bonuses that unlock as you hire workers and build buildings. Place your engine token on the railroad starting space. Place your certificate marker at zero on the certificate track.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Herd Deck
walkthrough.text: Take your fourteen player cattle cards. These are your starting herd, mostly low-value cattle. Shuffle them face down as your draw stack. Draw four cards into your hand. This is the deck you will improve throughout the game by buying better cattle from the market.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Objectives
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the four starting objective cards and deal one face up to each player. Return any leftovers to the box. Your starting objective gives you a personal scoring goal on top of the general objectives available to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Tile
walkthrough.text: If playing with three or four players, each player takes a player count tile and covers the Phase A section of their player board with the appropriate side. Two-player games skip this step. This tile adjusts movement rules for different player counts.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Money
walkthrough.text: Give each player starting money based on turn order. The first player gets six dollars. The second player gets seven. The third gets eight, and the fourth gets nine. The extra dollar compensates for acting later. Choose a starting player randomly and you are ready to hit the trail.

walkthrough.title: The Core Loop
walkthrough.text: Great Western Trail is all about one repeating cycle. You drive your cattle along the trail from Texas to Kansas City, stopping at locations to take actions along the way. When you reach Kansas City, you deliver your cattle by train to a city, earn money and points, then loop back to the start and do it again. You will make this journey about five to seven times during the game, getting stronger each trip.

walkthrough.title: Phase A: Move Your Cattleman
walkthrough.text: On your turn, the first thing you do is move your cattleman forward along the trail. You move a number of steps equal to your step limit, which starts at three or four depending on player count. Each location you pass or stop on costs one step. Empty trail spaces are not locations and do not cost steps. You must always end on a location. You can never pass Kansas City.

walkthrough.title: Paying Fees
walkthrough.text: Some locations charge fees when you pass through or stop on them. Hazard tiles and teepee locations charge fees paid to the bank. Another player's private building charges a fee paid directly to that player. The fee amount depends on the number of hand icons shown and the per-icon cost, which varies by player count. Plan your route to minimize fees when possible.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Take Actions
walkthrough.text: After moving, you take the actions available at your destination location. Neutral buildings and your own private buildings provide various actions. Some buildings let you buy cattle, hire workers, move your train, gain money, or build new private buildings. You typically get to perform several actions at a single location, making each stop count.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Refill Your Hand
walkthrough.text: At the end of your turn, draw cards from your herd deck up to your hand limit, which starts at four. If your draw stack is empty, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw stack. Your hand represents the cattle you are currently driving, and hand quality at Kansas City directly determines your delivery payout.

walkthrough.title: Buying Cattle
walkthrough.text: One of the most important actions is buying better cattle from the cattle market. Market cattle have higher values and different breeds than your starting herd. When you buy a cattle card, pay its cost in dollars and add it to your discard pile. Over time, these better cattle replace your weak starting cards, improving your Kansas City deliveries. Think of this as deck building.

walkthrough.title: Hiring Workers
walkthrough.text: Workers are hired from the job market and come in three specializations. Cowboys help with cattle-focused development, including buying cattle and increasing your hand limit. Craftsmen support building placement and point generation. Engineers advance your train along the railroad. When you hire a worker, place it beside your player board and unlock the bonus hidden under the disc it removes.

walkthrough.title: The Railroad
walkthrough.text: Your engine token tracks how far your train has advanced along the railroad. Moving your engine costs actions or money, but reaching station master tiles grants powerful bonuses. More importantly, your railroad position determines which cities you can deliver cattle to at Kansas City. A further engine opens higher-value delivery options.

walkthrough.title: Building Private Buildings
walkthrough.text: You can place your private building tiles on empty spaces along the trail. Once built, these buildings provide custom actions when you or others stop on them. When other players stop on your building, they must pay you a fee. This creates an economic pressure system where trail control matters. Each building has an A side and a B side with different actions.

walkthrough.title: Reaching Kansas City
walkthrough.text: When your cattleman reaches Kansas City, you must stop. This triggers a delivery. First, count the different breeds of cattle in your hand. Each unique breed adds to your delivery value. High-value cattle and diverse breeds mean bigger payouts. Then choose a city to deliver to, based on how far your train has advanced. Farther cities yield more money and victory points.

walkthrough.title: Delivery Resolution
walkthrough.text: When delivering, you compare your hand value to the city's requirements. You earn money equal to your delivery value, and you place one of your discs on the city to mark it. If your hand value does not meet the city's threshold, you may still deliver to a lower city. After delivery, discard your entire hand, draw a new one, and your cattleman resets to the trail start for the next loop.

walkthrough.title: Hazards and Teepees
walkthrough.text: Hazard tiles along the trail represent floods, droughts, and rockfalls. Landing on one costs you money or restricts your options. However, some actions let you remove hazards from the trail, which grants rewards and opens up cleaner paths for future loops. Teepee tiles connect to the Indian trade section and provide trading opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Disc Unlocking
walkthrough.text: As you hire workers and place buildings, you remove discs from your player board. Each disc you remove reveals a permanent bonus underneath. Some bonuses increase your hand limit. Others boost your step count, reduce costs, or provide ongoing income. This is the engine-building core of the game. The more discs you remove, the more powerful you become.

walkthrough.title: Certificates
walkthrough.text: The certificate track provides a way to improve your deliveries without upgrading your train. Moving your certificate marker up the track lets you add bonus value to deliveries. Certificates can be advanced through specific actions and building effects. They represent your reputation as a reliable cattle trader.

walkthrough.title: Foresight Spaces
walkthrough.text: The foresight row at the top of the board shows upcoming tiles that will enter the game. When certain triggers occur, tiles from the foresight row are placed on the trail. This gives you advance warning about what hazards, teepees, or workers are coming. Use this information to plan your route and hiring strategy.

walkthrough.title: Staged Tile Pipeline
walkthrough.text: Tiles enter the game in three stages, indicated by back numbers one, two, and three. Stage one tiles are weaker workers and milder hazards. Stage two brings mid-level options. Stage three introduces the most powerful workers and toughest hazards. This staged pipeline ensures the game ramps up in difficulty and opportunity over time.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends when a specific end-game condition is met, typically when a certain number of tiles have been exhausted or a player's disc placement triggers the final round. All victory points are tallied at game end. Nothing scores during the game itself.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up victory points from all sources. These include city deliveries, station master bonuses, private building placements, objective card completion, hazard removal rewards, and various disc-related bonuses. The player with the highest total wins. Every subsystem in the game contributes to your final score, so diversified strategies tend to perform well.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Trail
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Great Western Trail! We are going to walk through the first loop of the trail together, from your starting position all the way to Kansas City and your first delivery. This tutorial uses a two-player example, but the principles apply at any count. Let's saddle up.

walkthrough.title: Your Starting Position
walkthrough.text: On your very first turn, you place your cattleman on any neutral building and start at Phase B, skipping movement. This is the only time this happens. Look at the available neutral buildings near the start of the trail and pick one with useful actions. A building that lets you hire a worker or gain money is a good first stop.

walkthrough.title: Player One's First Action
walkthrough.text: Player one places their cattleman on neutral building B, which offers a hire worker action. They look at the job market and hire a cowboy for three dollars. The cowboy goes next to their player board, and they remove a disc from the cowboy column, revealing a bonus that increases their hand limit to five. Smart first move.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's First Action
walkthrough.text: Player two places their cattleman on a different neutral building. They choose to gain money and draw cards. Getting cash early means more buying power for cattle and workers on future turns. Player two also draws up to their hand limit to see what cattle they are working with.

walkthrough.title: Moving Along the Trail
walkthrough.text: From the second turn onward, you move your cattleman forward. Let's say your step limit is four. You want to reach a building with useful actions, but you also need to watch for fee locations. If a hazard tile sits two steps ahead, you will have to pay to pass through it. Plan your movement to maximize good stops and minimize fees.

walkthrough.title: Buying Your First Cattle
walkthrough.text: Player one stops at a location that lets them buy cattle from the market. They spot a value-three blue cattle card for five dollars. That is a big upgrade over the value-one starting cattle. They pay five dollars and add it to their discard pile. When this card shows up in their hand at Kansas City, it will significantly boost their delivery value.

walkthrough.title: Why Breed Diversity Matters
walkthrough.text: At delivery, you count unique breeds in your hand. Starting cattle are all the same breed. That blue cattle card player one just bought is a different breed, so it adds diversity. A hand with three different breeds is worth much more than a hand with one breed at the same total value. Always think about which breeds you are missing.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Rushing to Kansas City
walkthrough.text: Player two decides to rush straight to Kansas City with minimal stops. They arrive with only low-value starting cattle in hand and no breed diversity. Their delivery barely meets the weakest city's threshold. They earn a meager payout while player one is still building up a strong hand along the trail.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Before You Deliver
walkthrough.text: Rushing to Kansas City with a weak hand wastes your delivery. Take enough stops along the trail to buy cattle, hire workers, and build your engine before arriving. A strong delivery is worth far more than two weak ones. The extra actions compound across the entire game.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring the Railroad
walkthrough.text: Player two has been ignoring the railroad entirely, focusing only on cattle. When they arrive at Kansas City, they can only deliver to the nearest city because their train has not moved. The nearest city offers the lowest payout. Meanwhile, player one invested in an engineer and moved their train, unlocking a mid-level city worth twice as much.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Advance Your Train
walkthrough.text: The railroad is not optional. Your train position determines which cities you can deliver to. Higher cities pay more money and more victory points. Even one or two advances early on open significantly better delivery options. Do not neglect the railroad, even if your primary focus is cattle or buildings.

walkthrough.title: Player One's First Delivery
walkthrough.text: Player one arrives at Kansas City with a hand of four cards including two different breeds and one value-three cattle. Their delivery value qualifies for a mid-tier city. They earn eight dollars and place a disc on the city. Discard the hand, draw fresh cards, and reset the cattleman to the trail start. Loop one complete!

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Fee Leakage
walkthrough.text: On the next loop, player two repeatedly passes through player one's private building and hazard tiles. Each pass costs two or three dollars. Over several turns, they have leaked ten dollars in fees without even noticing. That is enough to have bought two cattle upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Fees
walkthrough.text: Fees add up fast. Before you move, count the fee locations along each possible path. Sometimes taking a slightly longer route avoids an expensive toll and saves you more than the extra steps cost. Especially watch out for other players' private buildings that feed money directly into their economy.

walkthrough.title: Starting the Second Loop
walkthrough.text: With the cattleman back at the start, loop two begins. This time you have more options. Maybe you hired a worker, bought better cattle, and moved your train. Each loop should be more efficient than the last because your engine has grown. Take smarter stops and aim for a bigger delivery.

walkthrough.title: Building a Private Building
walkthrough.text: Player one uses an action to place a private building tile on an empty trail space. They choose a spot along a popular path. Now every time player two passes through, they pay player one a fee. Plus, player one gets a custom action when they stop on their own building. Trail control is real.

walkthrough.title: Engine Growth
walkthrough.text: Notice how the game builds momentum. Your starting hand limit was four, but hiring has increased it to five. Your herd now has some strong cattle mixed in. Your train has advanced to better cities. Each improvement feeds the next. The player who builds their engine fastest pulls ahead in the mid-game.

walkthrough.title: Planning Ahead
walkthrough.text: Use the foresight row to see what tiles are coming. If a powerful engineer is entering the job market next round, plan to stop at a hiring location. If a nasty hazard is about to appear on your preferred path, consider an alternate route. Information is power in Great Western Trail.

walkthrough.title: Thinking About Objectives
walkthrough.text: Do not forget your objective cards. Check your starting objective and the four general objectives. These can be worth significant points at game end. If your objective rewards having cowboys, lean into cowboy hiring. If it rewards delivering to specific cities, plan your railroad accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have completed your first loop of the Great Western Trail. You know how to move along the trail, take actions at locations, buy cattle, hire workers, and deliver at Kansas City. Remember the key lessons. Build before you deliver. Advance your train for better cities. And watch your fees to protect your economy.

walkthrough.title: Before You Ride
walkthrough.text:
Before you play a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for route planning and economic fundamentals.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to worker types, building actions, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, let's cover the biggest beginner traps. First, rushing to Kansas City with a weak hand wastes your most valuable action. Second, ignoring the railroad locks you into the worst delivery cities. Third, paying excessive fees by taking careless routes drains your economy. Fourth, focusing on only one subsystem and neglecting others leaves scoring gaps. And fifth, forgetting about objective cards can cost you ten or more points at game end.

walkthrough.title: Build a Coherent First Loop
walkthrough.text: Your first loop sets the tone for the entire game. Plan stops that work together. Hire a worker who aligns with your intended strategy. Buy one or two cattle that improve your hand diversity. Move your train at least once. By the time you reach Kansas City on loop one, you should have the seeds of an engine growing. A scattered first loop with random actions will leave you behind.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Lane Early
walkthrough.text: Great Western Trail rewards commitment to a primary strategy lane. Cowboys power cattle-focused development. Craftsmen enable building-focused strategies. Engineers drive railroad advancement. By your second loop, you should have hired at least two workers of the same type. This focuses your disc removal and ensures the bonuses you unlock synergize with your actions.

walkthrough.title: Herd Quality Over Quantity
walkthrough.text: Your herd deck is a deck-building puzzle. It is better to have a lean deck with a few high-value, diverse cattle than a bloated deck full of weak cards. Every cattle card you buy should serve a purpose. Does it add a new breed for diversity? Does it have a high enough value to justify the cost? Avoid buying cattle just because you can.

walkthrough.title: Route Planning and Fee Management
walkthrough.text: Before moving each turn, scan the trail ahead. Count how many fee locations sit along each possible path. Sometimes the direct route costs six dollars in fees while a slightly longer path costs nothing. Every dollar saved on fees is a dollar available for cattle, workers, or buildings. Route planning is one of the most underrated skills in the game.

walkthrough.title: Disc Removal Is Your Engine
walkthrough.text: Removing discs from your player board is how your engine grows. Each disc reveals a permanent bonus: more steps, higher hand limit, extra income, or reduced costs. Prioritize disc removal early in the game because these bonuses compound across every remaining loop. The player who removes the most discs by mid-game has the strongest engine.

walkthrough.title: Balance Subsystems
walkthrough.text: While committing to a primary lane is important, do not completely ignore the other subsystems. A cattle-focused player still needs some railroad progress to access decent cities. A railroad-focused player still needs some cattle quality for meaningful deliveries. Check your scoring categories periodically and fill gaps before the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Objectives Are Not Optional
walkthrough.text: Objective cards are a major source of victory points. Your starting objective and the four general objectives can easily account for fifteen to twenty percent of your final score. Check them often and work toward them throughout the game. If a general objective aligns with your existing strategy, picking it up costs you very little extra effort for significant points.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't rush KC with weak hand — wastes your best action
Don't ignore railroad — locks you into worst cities
Don't leak fees — route planning protects your economy
Don't neglect objectives — worth 15-20% of final score

Most discs removed by mid-game = strongest engine

Check scoring categories and fill gaps before endgame

walkthrough.title: Cowboy Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The cowboy-focused strategy prioritizes cattle acquisition and deck quality. Hire cowboys early to increase your hand limit and unlock cattle-buying discounts. Buy diverse, high-value cattle to maximize delivery payouts. With a hand limit of six or seven and a deck full of premium cattle, every delivery becomes a windfall. The cowboy engine funds itself through superior Kansas City payouts.

walkthrough.title: Craftsman Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The craftsman-focused strategy centers on building placement. Hire craftsmen to unlock building slots and point bonuses. Place private buildings along popular routes to collect fees from opponents and create custom action stops for yourself. Craftsman bonuses often include direct victory points from disc removal. This strategy generates income passively through fee collection while scoring through building-related VP.

walkthrough.title: Engineer Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The engineer-focused strategy maximizes railroad advancement. Hire engineers to move your train faster and unlock station master bonuses. With an advanced train, you can deliver to the highest-value cities, earning far more per delivery than other players. Station master tiles provide powerful ongoing abilities. This strategy requires fewer cattle upgrades because the delivery city bonus compensates for hand quality.

walkthrough.title: Hybrid Approaches
walkthrough.text: Most winning strategies are not pure single-lane builds. A cowboy-engineer hybrid combines strong cattle hands with high-value delivery cities for maximum payouts. A craftsman-cowboy hybrid uses building fees to fund cattle upgrades. The key is to pick a primary lane and a supporting lane. Avoid spreading evenly across all three. Two strong lanes beat three weak ones.

walkthrough.title: Delivery Timing Optimization
walkthrough.text: Advanced players optimize how many loops they make and when. Taking slightly longer loops with more valuable stops can yield higher total scores than rushing through many quick deliveries. Calculate the opportunity cost of each extra stop versus reaching Kansas City sooner. In the late game, prioritize speed when there are few rounds remaining.

walkthrough.title: Trail Control and Fee Pressure
walkthrough.text: Placing buildings on high-traffic paths creates an income stream that scales with opponents' activity. In a four-player game, a well-placed building can earn you eight to twelve dollars across the full game. Meanwhile, opponents must decide whether to pay fees or take less efficient routes. This economic pressure is stronger at higher player counts where the trail is more congested.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Pivot
walkthrough.text: In the final two loops, shift your focus from engine building to point maximization. Complete any remaining objectives. Make your highest-value delivery with the best possible hand. Remove any remaining discs that score VP. The transition from building to scoring usually happens around loop four or five. Players who keep building past this point leave points on the table.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: Advanced players constantly read the board. Check which tiles are in the foresight row. Track which objective cards are still available. Notice where opponents are placing buildings and which delivery cities they are targeting. This information shapes your route choices, hiring priorities, and delivery timing. The player with the best information makes the best decisions.

summary.title: Cowboy Engine Strategy
bullets: Hire cowboys → +hand limit, cattle discounts
Buy diverse high-value cattle for max delivery payouts
Self-funding: superior KC payouts fuel further purchases

summary.title: Craftsman Engine Strategy
bullets: Hire craftsmen → building slots, VP bonuses from disc removal
Place buildings on popular routes for passive fee income
Economic pressure through trail control

summary.title: Engineer Engine Strategy
bullets: Hire engineers → faster train, station master bonuses
High-value cities compensate for moderate hand quality
Fewer cattle upgrades needed

summary.title: Hybrid Approaches
bullets: Cowboy-Engineer: strong hands + high-value cities = max payouts
Craftsman-Cowboy: building fees fund cattle upgrades
2 strong lanes > 3 weak ones

summary.title: Delivery Timing Optimization
bullets: Longer loops with better stops can outscore many quick deliveries
Calculate opportunity cost of extra stops vs KC speed
Late game: prioritize speed when rounds are limited

summary.title: Trail Control and Fee Pressure
bullets: Well-placed building = $8-12 income over full game
Opponents choose: pay fees or take inefficient routes
Stronger at higher player counts

summary.title: Endgame Pivot
bullets: Final 2 loops: shift from building to point maximization
Complete objectives, make best delivery, score remaining discs
Pivot around loop 4-5; later building wastes points

summary.title: Reading the Board State
bullets: Check foresight row, available objectives, opponent positions
Track enemy building placement and target cities
Best information → best decisions

entries.definition: Your player piece that moves along the trail from Texas toward Kansas City. Stops at locations to take actions. Resets to trail start after each delivery.
Your personal board tracking workers hired, buildings placed, and bonuses unlocked. Discs cover bonus spaces that are revealed as you hire and build.
Your personal deck of cattle cards. Consists of a draw stack, hand, and discard pile. Improved by buying market cattle. Hand quality at Kansas City determines delivery value.
Tracks your train's position on the railroad. Advancing the engine unlocks higher-value delivery cities and station master bonuses.
Tracks your position on the certificate track, which adds bonus value to deliveries. An alternative way to improve payouts without advancing the railroad.
14 colored discs placed on your player board at setup. Removed when hiring workers or building. Each removal reveals a permanent bonus underneath.
First phase of your turn. Move your cattleman forward along the trail up to your step limit. Each location passed or stopped on costs one step.
Second phase of your turn. Resolve the actions available at your destination location. Multiple actions may be available at one stop.
Third phase of your turn. Draw cards from your herd draw stack up to your hand limit.
Maximum number of locations you can pass through or stop at when moving. Starts at 3 or 4 depending on player count. Can be increased via disc bonuses.
Any space on the trail where your cattleman can stop: neutral buildings, private buildings, hazards, teepees, and Kansas City. Empty trail spaces are not locations.
Cost paid when passing through or stopping on certain locations. Hazard and teepee fees go to the bank. Private building fees go to the building owner.
Symbol on locations indicating a fee must be paid. The number of hand icons multiplied by the per-icon cost (varies by player count) determines the total fee.
Worker type supporting cattle-focused development. Hiring cowboys unlocks bonuses related to hand limit, cattle buying, and herd management.
Worker type supporting building-focused development. Hiring craftsmen unlocks bonuses related to building placement, cost reduction, and direct victory points.
Worker type supporting railroad-focused development. Hiring engineers unlocks bonuses related to train advancement and station access.
The area on the board where available workers are displayed for hiring. Workers enter from the staged tile pipeline and cost dollars to hire.
One of seven shared buildings (A-G) placed on the board during setup. Provide standard actions available to all players. No fees charged.
Player-owned building tiles placed on empty trail spaces. Provide custom actions for the owner and charge fees to other players who stop there. Each has A and B sides.
Display of market cattle cards available for purchase. Higher-value and more diverse cattle improve delivery payouts. Refilled based on player count when triggered.
The number of different cattle breeds (colors) in your hand at delivery. More unique breeds increase delivery value significantly. Key scoring driver.
Triggered when reaching Kansas City. Count unique breeds and total value in hand, choose a delivery city based on train position, earn money and place a disc.
The end point of the trail where deliveries are made. Movement must stop here. After delivery, cattleman resets to trail start for the next loop.
Track along the top of the board where your engine token advances. Further advancement unlocks higher-value delivery cities and station master bonuses.
Tiles placed on railroad stations during setup. Provide powerful bonuses when your engine reaches their station. Five stations total.
Obstacles on the trail representing floods, droughts, and rockfalls. Landing on or passing through charges fees. Can be removed for rewards via specific actions.
Tiles connecting to the Indian trade section. Come in blue and green varieties. Landing provides trading opportunities and potential bonuses.
Display showing upcoming tiles that will enter the game. Contains two tiles each from back-1, back-2, and back-3 piles. Provides advance planning information.
Number (1, 2, or 3) on the back of worker, hazard, and teepee tiles indicating their stage. Stage 1 enters first (weakest), stage 3 last (strongest).
Scoring cards showing end-game conditions worth victory points. Four general objectives available to all players, plus one starting objective per player.
All VP scored at game end from deliveries, buildings, station masters, objectives, hazard removal, and disc bonuses. Highest total wins.
Maximum number of cattle cards you can hold. Starts at 4. Increased by hiring cowboys and removing specific discs from your player board.
When your herd draw stack is empty and you need to draw, shuffle your discard pile to form a new draw stack. Then continue drawing.
Permanent bonus revealed when a disc is removed from your player board. Includes increased step limit, hand limit, income, cost reduction, and direct VP.
One complete journey from the trail start to Kansas City. Players typically complete 5-7 loops per game, with each loop being stronger than the last.
Tile covering Phase A of player board in 3-4 player games. Adjusts movement rules for different player counts. Not used in 2-player games.
